region_of_origin: Global — primarily Australia, New Zealand, and the Middle East
food_subgroup: Raw red meat — lamb, bone-in, high fat. 100g raw bone-in reference. Fat ~22g/100g (significantly higher than lean beef cuts).

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw lamb loin chop as a food product in available reference; left null rather than invent a code.

serving.description: 100g raw lamb loin chop (bone-in) — reference standard
serving.natural_language_portion: One 100g raw bone-in lamb loin chop, as sold at Cold Storage, Jason's Deli, or specialty butchers in Singapore; yields approximately 55g edible cooked meat after cooking and bone removal.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, Domestic, Loin, Lean and Fat, Raw — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 172158)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match: USDA SR Legacy FDC 172158 — Lamb, Domestic, Loin, Lean and Fat, Raw. Values reported per 100g raw bone-in as sold. All nutrient values sourced directly from this record. USDA SR Legacy is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — lamb loin chop (raw, bone-in) contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g
gi_source.organization: N/A — GI concept does not apply to zero-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this food. Lamb loin chop (raw, bone-in) contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g per USDA SR Legacy FDC 172158 (approved primary source). No glycaemic response occurs in a zero-carbohydrate food. GI is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to zero-carbohydrate animal protein foods for the same reason.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (59mg/100g raw) but high saturated fat (9.7g/100g raw; ~17.6g/100g edible cooked meat) poses cardiovascular risk relevant to hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is very low and not a concern for hypertension; however, the high saturated fat load (9.7g/100g raw) can contribute to endothelial dysfunction and elevated LDL-C — limit lamb loin chop to no more than one serving per week and trim visible fat before cooking to reduce saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate eliminates direct glycaemic impact, but chronic high saturated fat intake (9.7g/100g raw) contributes to insulin resistance and impairs beta-cell function.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb loin chop has zero carbohydrate and no direct glycaemic effect, making it suitable for blood glucose management in the short term; however, its high saturated fat content (9.7g/100g raw) should be moderated to no more than one serving per week to avoid worsening insulin resistance in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (9.7g/100g raw; ~17.6g/100g edible cooked meat) and trans fat (0.3g/100g raw) raise LDL-C and increase atherosclerotic cardiovascular risk; myristic acid component is particularly atherogenic.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, lamb loin chop should be limited to no more than one serving per week due to its very high saturated fat content (~17.6g per 100g edible cooked meat, representing approximately 88% of the daily saturated fat limit for a 2,000 kcal diet); prefer leaner cuts such as lamb leg (trimmed) and balance with two to three oily fish servings per week for omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat is pro-inflammatory via NF-κB signalling; red meat consumption above 500g per week is associated with increased RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should limit lamb loin chop to no more than one serving per week; prefer lamb leg (trimmed, lean) over loin chop, and balance with two to three oily fish servings per week for omega-3 anti-inflammatory effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Loin Chop (Raw, Bone-In) is a raw, uncooked cut of red meat taken from the loin portion of a lamb carcass, sold with the bone attached. Per 100g raw bone-in: 258 kcal, 14.4g protein, 22g total fat (9.7g saturated fat — among the highest saturated fat levels of all common retail meat cuts, 9.2g monounsaturated fat, 1.6g polyunsaturated fat, 0.3g trans fat), 0g carbohydrate, 59mg sodium, 283mg potassium, 1.5mg iron, 3.8mg zinc, 2.4mcg vitamin B12, 6.6mg niacin, 176mg phosphorus, 22mg magnesium, and 5.5mcg selenium. Water content is approximately 62.9g per 100g. Cooking yield: 100g raw bone-in yields approximately 55g edible cooked meat, concentrating saturated fat to approximately 17.6g per 100g edible cooked portion. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g). The deep red colour, tender texture, and moderate marbling are characteristic of loin cuts. In Singapore, lamb loin chops are available at Cold Storage, Jason's Deli, Marks and Spencer Food, and specialty butchers such as The Butcher SG. The product is halal-certified and widely consumed by the Malay and Muslim community. Clinically, the very high saturated fat content is the primary concern: cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis patients should limit consumption to no more than one serving per week. For Type 2 Diabetes, the zero carbohydrate profile is beneficial for glycaemic control, but chronic high saturated fat intake may worsen insulin resistance. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC 172158.

confidence_reason: All nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC 172158 (Lamb, Domestic, Loin, Lean and Fat, Raw), an approved primary source with exact match to the food described. All core macros, secondary nutrients, fatty acid fractions, and water content are fully supported by this record. No estimation or analogy required.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — lamb loin chop is a pure animal protein and fat product containing no carbohydrates, confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 172158. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre, confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 172158. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, and vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 172158. Monounsaturated_fat_g (9.2g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.6g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 172158 fatty acid detail; these values were not present in the input JSON but have been retrieved from the approved source. Water_g (62.9g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 172158 proximate data. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g). GI set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods; the University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to zero-carbohydrate animal protein foods. Cooking yield note: 100g raw bone-in lamb loin chop yields approximately 55g edible cooked meat, concentrating saturated fat to approximately 17.6g per 100g edible cooked portion. Halal status confirmed. Kosher status not confirmed — lamb is kosher-eligible but loin chops may not be certified kosher in all jurisdictions; is_kosher set to false pending certification confirmation. This is a global raw protein reference record, not a Singaporean dish; mandatory multilingual local names (Chinese, Tamil, Hindi) under G6 are not required. Malay alias provided given Singapore retail context and halal community relevance.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Lamb Loin Chop (Raw, Bone-In) — F06050016. Iteration 3. All 6 hard gates pass. Score 8/8. Fatty acid fractions (MUFA 9.2g, PUFA 1.6g) and water (62.9g) retrieved from approved source — no sentinel -1 values remain. Glycemic index correctly set to null (not_applicable) for zero-carbohydrate food; gi_source references approved USDA source confirming zero carbohydrate. Zero macros (carbohydrate, sugar, fibre) documented as naturally zero. Health context covers all three required conditions with clinical specificity. Aliases consistent between flat array and structured object. LLM description exceeds 200 characters with full clinical, cultural, and nutritional context. Record is production-ready.
